messages.content:
Hi everyone, we have now published the GitHub repository for the official Stage 1 evaluation setup for the Mathematics Distillation Challenge: Equational Theories, together with the current planned evaluation models:

https://github.com/SAIRcompetition/equational-theories-stage1-judge

The current planned Stage 1 evaluation models are OpenAI’s GPT-OSS-120B, Meta’s Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, and Google’s Gemma-4-31B-IT. These models are currently planned to serve as the final Stage 1 evaluation models, with equal weight, though the setup may still be adjusted based on community feedback.

The repository also lets participants test prompts locally before submission.

Thanks to everyone for participating in the challenge and for the helpful discussion and feedback.
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content: Hi! Does the playground now use the same model configs as the official eval (temperature 0.0, pinned providers, reasoning disabled for Llama)? Want to make sure local testing matches final evaluation.
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
Hi all, while validating our cheatsheet against the strict config in https://github.com/SAIRcompetition/equational-theories-stage1-judge/blob/main/evaluation_models.json, we hit a consistent silent failure on gemma-4-31b-it. Sharing findings and two small suggestions in case anyone else is chasing the same symptom.

What we see is that by reproducing the exact payload judge.py builds for gemma on a hard1 problem with our ~10 KB cheatsheet:                     
  {                                                                                                                          
    "model": "google/gemma-4-31b-it",
    "max_tokens": 8192, "seed": 0, "temperature": 0.0,
    "reasoning": {"effort": "low"},                                                                                          
    "provider": {"order": ["Novita"], "quantizations": ["bf16"], "allow_fallbacks": false}
  }                                                                                                                          

what comes back:                                                                                                           

{
    "choices": [{
      "message": {"content": null, "reasoning": "...<filled>..."},
      "finish_reason": "length"
    }],
    "usage": {"completion_tokens": 8192}                                                                                     
  }

=> content is empty, the full 8192-token budget is consumed by hidden reasoning, and the model is cut off mid-thought. It reproduces on every hard1 problem we try.

So we've got two suggestions:

(A) Drop reasoning_effort for gemma-4-31b-it. In control runs gemma produces cleaner, stabler verdicts without thinking mode on this task, and with reasoning enabled the failure is silent (see below).

OR

(B) Raise gemma's max_output_tokens cap when reasoning is enabled (e.g. 16384), AND fix the doubled-budget retry in llm.py. The existing guardrail: retry_max = min(max_tokens * 2, profile.max_output_tokens) if retry_max > max_tokens:  # never True for gemma: both sides are 8192 is a no-op because profile.max_output_tokens == initial max_tokens == 8192, so the retry path whose entire purpose is this failure mode is unreachable.                                                                                               

Two observations that tighten the diagnosis: 1. Without reasoning, the same config works cleanly. By dropping just reasoning.effort="low" and leaving everything else identical, we consistently got 52/69 correct on hard1, 0 parse errors, taking ~11 min at concurrency 10; 2. Minimal repro is confirming reasoning is the culprit, as the same request on x = y * x → x * y = y * x: took 30 s, 103 reasoning tokens, 0 content tokens, finish_reason=length.            

Why I'm flagging it: reasoning.effort="low" enables gemma's thinking mode, and reasoning tokens are billed against max_tokens like completion  tokens. With 8192 − ~2500 prompt tokens of headroom, hard problems exhaust the budget before any visible verdict lands. The config then returns HTTP 200 with an empty string and no explicit error, so any harness that doesn't check finish_reason + reasoning_tokens sees it as a parse failure. I spent a few hours thinking it was a Novita bf16 provider outage before the interaction clicked, so I want to spare the next person that debugging session...
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

What we see is that by reproducing the exact payload judge.py builds for gemma on a hard1 problem with our ~10 KB cheatsheet:

    "max_tokens": 8192, "seed": 0, "temperature": 0.0,
    "reasoning": {"effort": "low"},                                                                                          
    "provider": {"order": ["Novita"], "quantizations": ["bf16"], "allow_fallbacks": false}
  }

  {
    "choices": [{
      "message": {"content": null, "reasoning": "...<filled>..."},
      "finish_reason": "length"
    }],
    "usage": {"completion_tokens": 8192}                                                                                     
  }

 => content is empty, the full 8192-token budget is consumed by hidden reasoning, and the model is cut off mid-thought. It reproduces on every hard1 problem we try.

 So we've got two suggestions:

  (A) **Drop reasoning_effort for gemma-4-31b-it in evaluation_models.json**. In control runs gemma produces cleaner, stabler verdicts without thinking mode on this task, and with reasoning enabled the failure is silent (see below).

**OR**

 (B) **Raise gemma's max_output_tokens cap when reasoning is enabled** (e.g. 16384), AND **fix the doubled-budget retry in llm.py**. The existing guardrail: retry_max = min(max_tokens * 2, profile.max_output_tokens) if retry_max > max_tokens:  # never True for gemma: both sides are 8192 is a no-op because profile.max_output_tokens == initial max_tokens == 8192, so the retry path whose entire purpose is this failure mode is unreachable.                                                                                               

Two observations that tighten the diagnosis: 1. **Without reasoning, the same config works cleanly**. By dropping just reasoning.effort="low" and leaving everything else identical, we consistently got 52/69 correct on hard1, 0 parse errors, taking ~11 min at concurrency 10; 2. **Minimal repro is confirming reasoning is the culprit**, as the same request on x = y * x → x * y = y * x:  took 30 s, 103 reasoning tokens, 0 content tokens, finish_reason=length.             

 **Why I'm flagging it**: reasoning.effort="low" enables gemma's thinking mode, and reasoning tokens are billed against max_tokens like completion  tokens. With 8192 − ~2500 prompt tokens of headroom, hard problems exhaust the budget before any visible verdict lands. The config then returns HTTP 200 with an empty string and no explicit error, so any harness that doesn't check finish_reason + reasoning_tokens sees it as a parse failure. I spent a few hours thinking it was a Novita bf16 provider outage before the interaction clicked, so I want to spare the next person that debugging session...

With 8192 − ~2500 prompt tokens of headroom, hard problems exhaust the budget before any visible verdict lands. The config then returns HTTP 200 with an empty string and no explicit error, so any harness that doesn't check finish_reason + reasoning_tokens sees it as a parse failure. I spent a few hours thinking it was a Novita bf16 provider outage before the interaction clicked, so I want to spare the next person that debugging session...

messages.content:
Garbid said:

Hi! Does the playground now use the same model configs as the official eval (temperature 0.0, pinned providers, reasoning disabled for Llama)? Want to make sure local testing matches final evaluation.

@Garbid We will check and get back to you asap. Thanks!
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
@Erzio Thanks for the great feedback and suggestions.
Gemma4-31b-it was just released by Google last week and has quickly become a very popular model. We think it is a great model for this competition. At the same time, the current OpenRouter/provider workflow and configuration for this route are still not fully stable. We are now reviewing the setup more carefully, including the reasoning setting, token cap, and provider routing, to determine a more reliable configuration.
As noted in the policy, the planned evaluation setup may be adjusted based on community feedback, and we will optimize the evaluation setup based on suggestions from the community.

messages.content:
@Erzio Thanks for the feedback and suggestions.

We have updated the planned Stage 1 evaluation setup on GitHub and disabled reasoning for Gemma-4-31B-IT in the current configuration: https://github.com/SAIRcompetition/equational-theories-stage1-judge

We will also continue monitoring provider stability for this model on OpenRouter and will keep an eye on whether a more stable provider becomes available.
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
Hi everyone, with 5 days remaining in Stage 1 of the Mathematics Distillation Challenge - Equational Theories, we wanted to share a few updates:

1. Evaluation configuration
Thank you for the helpful feedback from the community. Stage 1 evaluation will include both the 8192 and 16384 output-token settings, with separate leaderboards for each. The official evaluation setup is here: https://github.com/SAIRcompetition/equational-theories-stage1-judge
For gemma-4-31b-it, the current issue is provider stability on OpenRouter. If this remains unresolved by the end of Stage 1, we may use a SAIR-hosted serving setup for evaluation of gemma-4-31b-it, with the same model and fixed configuration, while ensuring fairness of the evaluation.

2. Final evaluation problem set
The final offline evaluation set will be completely different from the public selected problem sets, and it will include different levels of difficulty. We strongly encourage participants not to overfit to specific public subsets, but instead to focus on underlying mathematical reasoning and generalization.

3. Contributor Network
We encourage participants to share prompts through the SAIR Contributor Network. Shared prompts can be benchmarked and shown on the leaderboard here: https://competition.sair.foundation/contributor-network?competition=mathematics-distillation-challenge-equational-theories-stage1&tab=leaderboard
This may help participants iterate and improve more effectively. We also want to thank community members who have shared github repos, experience reports, and papers. We really appreciate this open collaboration.

4. About Stage 2
Stage 2 will begin after Stage 1 ends. After internal discussion, we have adjusted participation so that Stage 2 will be open to anyone who wants to register. Stage 1 participants will be able to use the Stage 2 Playground. More detailed rules will be announced after Stage 2 begins.

Thank you again to everyone participating in SAIR competitions and contributing to the discussion.
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content: Hi @YZ we have run some few tests on different equations sets, to check for potential overfitting. One issue that emerged, is that models really struggle on equations of order equal or higher than 5. Can you confirm that the final test is going to be run on orders strictly lower than 5? more generally, what will the conditions of the set of equations of the final test? Thanks!
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
Erzio said:

Hi YZ we have run some few tests on different equations sets, to check for potential overfitting. One issue that emerged, is that models really struggle on equations of order equal or higher than 5. Can you confirm that the final test is going to be run on orders strictly lower than 5? more generally, what will the conditions of the set of equations of the final test? Thanks!

@Erzio I will confirm with the other co-organizers and get back to you. Thanks!
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
@Erzio Our current plan is that the Stage 1 final evaluation set will not include order 5 or higher problems. More generally, the final offline evaluation set will be different from the public selected problem sets and will include different levels of difficulty. The main goal is to evaluate generalization rather than performance on a specific subset.

We might also run a separate leaderboard on some order 5 problems as an interesting experiment, even if it is not part of the official leaderboard.
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
Here to share an observation that I forgot to share, regardless of whether it will affect the final config of the stage1 eval pipeline.

Last week I was curious of why the competition did not include DeepSeek-V3.2 as one of the target model. Because based on the result from Artificial Analysis:
image.png

DeepSeek-V3.2 sits at a balanced sweat spot between intelligence and cost, even better than gpt-oss-120b.

When I check the official benchmark , the numbers reported for DeepSeek-V3.2 was really low, despite having high 'Mean Parse Success'(which means likely the answers were not cut off due to limited output token budge)

I proceeded to conduct my own test with the default setting (no cheatsheet official prompt)and got this result:
image.png

It's a actually a draw with gpt-oss-120b, if not better.
Therefore I believe some of the values reported by the benchmark might not be 100% accurate.

One shortcoming of DeepSeek-V3.2 is that its throughput (token/s) is much lower than gpt-oss-120b. This should be mainly due to the fact that it is much less sparse a model than the latter.

However, this week, we added gemma-4-31B-it as one of the target model, which according to numbers reported by OpenRouter, has even lower throughput than DeepSeek-V3.2:
image.png
image.png

Maybe there are some reason that I'm not aware of that leads to the competition not shortlisting DeepSeek-V3.2, e.g. it's be too big a model to have a plan B (self-host), which is completely valid.

But just that the numbers reported on the official benchmark does not tally with my local test results. I'm curious if anyone else had similar observations?

messages.content: Hi @YZ may I check what is the --call-timeout value that the competition will use when doing the final official evaluation for stage1? Is it the default value (240)? Thanks.
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content: @Gan W. No, I think the --call-timeout value appears in the examples/ smoke-test runner and is intended only for local testing by participants. It is included there to make local evaluation more practical and to keep one slow call from blocking an entire local run. It is not part of the official Stage 1 evaluation setup.
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
YZ said:

keep

Thanks for the reply.

Eh does that mean for the final official eval there will not be limit on the --call-timeout?
currently it seems this can be achieved by setting a negative value:
image.png

Meaning no slow call will ever be registered as UNPARSED (thus wrong answer)?
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content: @Gan W. That is a very good question. Our current view is that it is generally not ideal to impose a strict per-problem timeout in the official evaluation, because that can end up reflecting provider-side latency or instability rather than the actual quality of the prompt or the model’s reasoning on the task. In practice, the fixed max_tokens cap already places a meaningful bound on each call: once the generation reaches that token limit, it stops. So it provides a more natural constraint for the evaluation setting.

YZ said:

Hi everyone, with 5 days remaining in Stage 1 of the Mathematics Distillation Challenge - Equational Theories, we wanted to share a few updates:

For gemma-4-31b-it, the current issue is provider stability on OpenRouter. If this remains unresolved by the end of Stage 1, we may use a SAIR-hosted serving setup for evaluation of gemma-4-31b-it, with the same model and fixed configuration, while ensuring fairness of the evaluation.

2. Final evaluation problem set

We strongly encourage participants not to overfit to specific public subsets, but instead to focus on underlying mathematical reasoning and generalization.

3. Contributor Network
We encourage participants to share prompts through the SAIR Contributor Network. Shared prompts can be benchmarked and shown on the leaderboard here: https://competition.sair.foundation/contributor-network?competition=mathematics-distillation-challenge-equational-theories-stage1&tab=leaderboard

Thank you again to everyone participating in SAIR competitions and contributing to the discussion.

Would you mind clarifying how the Stage 1 winners will be chosen? My current understanding is, assuming cheatsheets don't exceed an average cost of USD 0.01 per problem or a solve time of 10 minutes per problem, the winner will be determined by highest overall accuracy on a set of unpublished problems that are very similar to the public selected stage 2 subsets, and will contain an equal number of normal and hard problems.

I suspect my understanding isn't completely accurate. Can you clarify?
messages.subject: Stage 1 evaluation models and setup

messages.content:
Thanks everyone for the helpful feedback and questions. Let me give one unified reply on the current plan for the final evaluation set and scoring.

Our current plan is that all scored implications will carry equal weight.

For scoring, each problem will be evaluated under each evaluation model, and each model/problem pair will be run 3 times. So the total scored evaluations will be:
(# of scored problems) × (# of evaluation models) × (repeats)

In the current design, that would be:
600 × 3 × 3 = 5400

More specifically, the scored final evaluation set is currently planned as follows. The final evaluation set will be completely different from the public problem sets.

  • Normal: 200 problems, with 100 TRUE and 100 FALSE, at roughly the difficulty level of the current public normal subset.

  • Hard: 200 problems, with 100 TRUE and 100 FALSE, at roughly the difficulty level of the current public hard1 / hard2 subsets.

  • Extra hard: 200 problems, with 100 TRUE and 100 FALSE, at roughly the difficulty level of the current public hard3 subset.

All scored implications in the final evaluation are currently planned to be of order at most 4.

In addition, we are considering a separate order-5 set consisting of 200 order-5 implications, with 100 TRUE and 100 FALSE. This order-5 set would not be part of the official scored evaluation, but would instead have a separate leaderboard for research purposes.

Also, each team may submit only one cheatsheet, and the final submitted version will be the one used in evaluation. This follows the same general logic as academic paper submission, where the final submitted version is the version that is evaluated.
